meta.summary: Read-only performance & data-access audit of the IntelliFlow CRM monorepo. Prior N+1 remediation (IFC-314 / ADR-053) is real and validated: `pnpm nplus1:scan` shows 26 baselined/justified residuals, but the same scan surfaced 1 NEW unbaselined violation (apps/api/src/modules/email/inbound.router.ts) that ships undetected because the scanner is not wired into CI or scripts/pre-ship.mjs. Schema.prisma (5852 lines) is heavily and mostly correctly indexed (tenantId-prefixed composite indexes on Lead/Contact/Opportunity/Task etc.), but every ILIKE/`contains`-mode text search across Lead, Contact, Account, Opportunity, and the cross-entity global-search router runs against plain btree indexes (or no index at all) with no pg_trgm/GIN support anywhere in the DB (only uuid-ossp and vector extensions are enabled) — a real, growing full-scan risk as tables grow. The dashboard/home hot path issues 11 parallel COUNT/findUnique queries per load with zero caching and a dev-only console.warn slow-query signal that never reaches the OTel pipeline ADR-053 already wired for query counts. Two external HTTP calls (Gemini embeddings, OCR document fetch) lack an explicit request-level timeout/AbortController. Web bundle risk (recharts eager-imported in 13 files, 2 lazy-split) is grep-confirmed but its actual KB impact is UNAVAILABLE without a build.
unavailableMetrics: Exact web bundle size delta / route JS payload (PERF-011) — requires `pnpm --filter @intelliflow/web build` + bundle analyzer; not run per audit-only, no-build constraint.

p99/p95 latency of the searches flagged in PERF-003/PERF-004 under production-scale row counts — requires a running Postgres instance with representative data + EXPLAIN ANALYZE; not available in a static, read-only audit.

Actual production query-budget breach telemetry (whether PERF-005's 11-query dashboard block combines with sibling procedures to exceed the 15-query default in a single browser page load) — requires runtime tracing (x-query-count headers) against a live session, not available statically.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `node tools/scripts/nplus1-scan.mjs` output: '[nplus1-scan] 1 NEW high-confidence N+1 violation(s) (not in baseline): apps/api/src/modules/email/inbound.router.ts:946 [prisma-in-map] prisma.emailRecord.count'. Code at inbound.router.ts:911-970 (`getUnreadCounts` procedure): `const countQueries = folders.map(async (folder) => { ... await Promise.all([prisma.emailRecord.count(...), prisma.emailRecord.count(...)]) ... return { folder, count: await prisma.emailRecord.count({ where }) }; }); const counts = await Promise.all(countQueries);`. tools/scripts/nplus1-baseline.json:9 records only 1 occurrence of this signature; the scanner's AST pass now counts 3 (lines 946, 947, 962), i.e. actual occurrences exceed the accepted baseline.
findings.impact: For the default 5-folder input (`['inbox','sent','drafts','trash','spam']`), the procedure issues 6 separate `COUNT` round-trips per call (2 for inbox + 1 each for the other 4) instead of a single grouped aggregate. Because the scanner isn't wired into CI (see rootCause), this new fan-out shipped without a regression test or reviewer signal, and further growth (e.g. adding more folder types) would silently keep adding round-trips.
findings.rootCause: The inbox-vs-other-folders logic was extended (see the inline comment block at inbound.router.ts:935-943 explaining the two-count subtraction approach for inbox) without collapsing the per-folder counts into one `groupBy`/raw-SQL query. Separately, `nplus1:scan` (package.json:51) is not referenced by any `.github/workflows/*.yml` nor by `scripts/pre-ship.mjs` — confirmed via `grep -rln "nplus1:scan" .github/workflows/*.yml package.json scripts/pre-ship.mjs` returning only `package.json` — so a NEW violation like this has no automated gate to catch it before merge.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-053-n-plus-one-query-budget-detector.md (scanner exists specifically to catch this class of regression; its baseline contract at nplus1-baseline.json:2 states 'CI fails on any NEW signature' — but CI does not currently invoke the scanner, so that contract is not actually enforced)
findings.remediation: Replace the per-folder `.map()` fan-out with a single `prisma.emailRecord.groupBy({ by: ['status'] , where: { tenantId, isRead: false }, _count: true })`-style aggregate (or one raw SQL `GROUP BY` over the metadata JSON flags), computing all folder buckets from one result set. Separately, wire `pnpm nplus1:scan` into a CI job (or scripts/pre-ship.mjs) so future NEW violations fail the gate as ADR-053 intends, rather than only being visible via manual `nplus1:scan` runs.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `node tools/scripts/nplus1-scan.mjs` reports '26 N+1 finding(s) across 21 site(s); 23 baselined' (23 baselined signatures matching pre-existing counts, PERF-001's 3 occurrences vs 1 baselined = the +2 delta accounting for the 26th). tools/scripts/nplus1-baseline.json:2 documents each residual's justification category (bounded-Low settings upserts, intentional per-aggregate domain writes to preserve domain events, intentional pagination/depth-capped recursion). Cross-checked against docs/operations/nplus1-audit/NPLUS1_REMEDIATION_REPORT.md (referenced from ADR-053-n-plus-one-query-budget-detector.md:12).
findings.impact: No new risk — this is the existing, already-signed-off N+1 debt inventory (e.g. packages/adapters/src/audit/DurableAuditLogAdapter.ts:238,242 per-event audit-log writes; packages/application/src/services/TaskService.ts:607 per-task save; packages/adapters/src/repositories/PrismaAccountRepository.ts:208,214 depth<=5 capped ancestor walk). Recorded here only to close the audit scope item 'N+1 queries' with an explicit statement that these are known and accepted, not undiscovered.
findings.rootCause: Deliberate architectural tradeoff: batching the per-aggregate `.save()`/`.create()` calls in these 23 sites would require either raw SQL `ON CONFLICT` batch upserts or would break the emission of one domain event per aggregate — both judged higher-risk than the bounded, small-N query fan-out per tools/scripts/nplus1-baseline.json:2.
findings.remediation: No action required for this audit; if a future task revisits DurableAuditLogAdapter/OutboxEventBusAdapter batch-write ergonomics (e.g. Postgres `unnest()`-based bulk insert that still emits one event per row via a returned id list), re-run `nplus1:scan --update-baseline` to shrink the residual set.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -rn "CREATE EXTENSION" infra/supabase/migrations/*.sql` returns only `uuid-ossp` (20250101000000_initial_schema.sql:9) and `vector` (20250101000000_initial_schema.sql:10) — no `pg_trgm`. Every text-search filter in the app uses Prisma `{ contains: X, mode: 'insensitive' }`, which Postgres compiles to `column ILIKE '%X%'` (leading wildcard). Confirmed call sites: global-search.router.ts:67-249 (lead/contact/account/opportunity/ticket/task, all `contains`+`insensitive`), apps/api/src/modules/lead/lead.router.ts:129-132 & 1450-1453 (email/firstName/lastName/company), apps/api/src/modules/contact/contact.router.ts:162-166,172,1372-1374,1447-1453,1458 (email/firstName/lastName/title/department), apps/api/src/modules/account/account.router.ts:614-617,622,916-918,923 (name/website/industry/description), apps/api/src/modules/opportunity/opportunity.router.ts:425-426,876-877,1052-1053 (name/description/account.name). A leading-wildcard ILIKE cannot use a standard B-tree index (Postgres btree only accelerates prefix `LIKE 'X%'`); it always requires a sequential scan unless a `pg_trgm` GIN/GiST index exists. Contrast with packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:1589-1590, which explicitly notes a GIN tsvector index was added for MessageRecord full-text search as a 'reviewer follow-up' — the same treatment was never applied to Lead/Contact/Account/Opportunity/Ticket search.
findings.impact: Every free-text search (global search, and the `search` param on each entity's list endpoint) performs a full sequential scan of the tenant's rows in that table today; this scales linearly with tenant data volume. Under ADR-018's k6 budget (p95 <200ms) this is currently masked by low seed-data row counts, but is a straightforward regression as tenants accumulate real Lead/Contact/Account/Opportunity/Ticket rows — with no index to fall back on, sequential-scan cost grows unbounded rather than gracefully degrading.
findings.rootCause: Prisma's `contains`+`mode:'insensitive'` was used as a drop-in text-search primitive without provisioning the pg_trgm extension/GIN index it requires to stay indexed; the pattern was copied across 6 routers (5 entity routers + global-search) rather than centralized behind a single trigram-backed search helper.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-018-performance-load-testing.md (p99 <100ms core-endpoint / p95 <200ms k6 budget — at risk as data scales); ADR-053 does not cover this class (it targets query COUNT, not per-query cost)
findings.remediation: Enable `pg_trgm` in a new Supabase migration (`CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_trgm;`) and add `CREATE INDEX ... USING gin (column gin_trgm_ops)` (raw SQL, since Prisma schema.prisma cannot express trigram GIN natively — same pattern already used for MessageRecord's tsvector GIN per schema.prisma:1590) on Lead(email,firstName,lastName,company), Contact(email,firstName,lastName,title,department), Account(name,website,industry), Opportunity(name,description), and Ticket(subject,description). Re-verify with `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` that the ILIKE predicates switch from Seq Scan to Bitmap Index Scan post-migration.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: Lead model indexes at packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:487-497 are `email, status, score, ownerId, tenantId, createdAt` plus 4 tenantId-prefixed composites and `accountId` — no index on `firstName`, `lastName`, or `company`, all three of which are filtered in apps/api/src/modules/lead/lead.router.ts:130-132 (`buildLeadListWhere`'s `OR` search clause). Contact model indexes at schema.prisma:662-672 are `email, ownerId, accountId, tenantId` plus composites — no index on `firstName`, `lastName`, `title`, or `department`, all filtered at apps/api/src/modules/contact/contact.router.ts:163-166,172. Opportunity model indexes at schema.prisma:897-913 have no index at all on `name` or `description`, both filtered at apps/api/src/modules/opportunity/opportunity.router.ts:425-426.
findings.impact: Independent of the pg_trgm gap in PERF-003 (which affects the `contains` operator specifically), these columns have zero index coverage of any kind, so even an equality or prefix filter on them (e.g. a future `startsWith` search variant, or `department` exact-match at contact.router.ts:171-172 `if (department) baseWhere.department = ...`) would force a sequential scan.
findings.rootCause: Indexing effort was concentrated on `tenantId`-prefixed filter/sort columns (per the IFC-030/HOME-PERF/PG-059 comments visible at schema.prisma:493-496) used by list/dashboard queries, but the free-text `OR` search columns on Lead/Contact/Opportunity were not covered by the same pass.
findings.policyViolation: packages/db/CLAUDE.md (tenantId + query-pattern indexing discipline implied by existing composite-index comments in schema.prisma)
findings.remediation: Fold into the PERF-003 pg_trgm migration (trigram GIN indexes on these exact columns double as this fix), or, if pg_trgm is deferred, add plain B-tree indexes on `Contact.department` (equality-filterable today) as a quick partial mitigation.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: home.router.ts:1405-1504 issues `await Promise.all([...])` containing 11 separate queries (`user.findUnique`, then 10x `.count()` across task/lead/appointment/opportunity for high-priority-tasks, overdue-tasks, new-leads-{yesterday,week,month}, total-leads, appointments-today, deals-closed-{this-week,last-week,this-month,last-month}) all scoped to a single `userId`+`tenantId`, none behind a cache. `grep -rn "cache|redis" apps/api/src/modules/home/home.router.ts` returns no matches, versus apps/api/src/lib/rbac.ts:36-82 which does implement an explicit 60s in-memory permission cache (`_permissionCache`) as a comparable precedent elsewhere in the same package.
findings.impact: This is the 'welcome summary' dashboard block (per the schema comment at packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:495 `// HOME-PERF: Welcome summary lead counts`, confirming it is a known/named hot path) — it re-runs 11 round-trips on every home-page load for every active user, with no time-boxed cache despite the underlying counts (e.g. 'deals closed last month') being immutable after the period ends and 'total leads' changing only occasionally.
findings.rootCause: The counts are computed inline per-request rather than cached; while each query is individually indexed (composite `[tenantId, ownerId, ...]` indexes exist per schema.prisma:494-496), the aggregate cost is 11x round-trip latency with no reuse across repeated navigations within the same session.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-018-performance-load-testing.md (p99 <100ms core-endpoint budget — 11 sequential-latency round-trips, even parallelized, raise tail latency risk vs a single cached read)
findings.remediation: Cache the per-user summary payload for a short TTL (e.g. 30-60s, matching the pattern already used in apps/api/src/lib/rbac.ts:36) keyed by `${tenantId}:${userId}:homeSummary`, invalidated on lead/task/opportunity mutations, or consolidate same-model counts (4 lead counts, 4 opportunity counts) into single `groupBy`/`aggregate` calls with `_count` per date bucket to cut round-trips from 11 to ~4 regardless of caching.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: home.router.ts:361-363 `const duration = performance.now() - startTime; if (duration > 200) { console.warn(...) }` — a raw `console.warn`, not routed through `packages/observability`. `grep -n "observability|metricHelpers|recordDatabaseQuery" apps/api/src/modules/home/home.router.ts` returns no matches, whereas ADR-053-n-plus-one-query-budget-detector.md:100-101 documents `reporter.ts` already emitting structured over-budget events via `logger` + OTel `metricHelpers.recordDatabaseQuery`, and ADR-053:22-26 notes `packages/observability/src/metrics.ts` declares the `intelliflow.db.query.count`/`.duration` instruments this endpoint could reuse.
findings.impact: Slow-path signal for the highest-traffic dashboard endpoint (home summary, hit on effectively every session) is only visible in local dev server stdout, not in production logs, traces, or the OTel metrics pipeline already built for exactly this purpose — a production p95 regression on this endpoint would not surface through existing observability.
findings.rootCause: The `>200ms` warning predates (or was written independently of) the ADR-053 observability wiring and was never migrated to use it.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-053-n-plus-one-query-budget-detector.md (existing `intelliflow.db.query.count`/`.duration` OTel instruments are the intended reuse point per ADR text, 'complements the existing query observability')
findings.remediation: Replace the `console.warn` with a call into `packages/observability`'s metric/log helper (mirroring `reporter.ts`'s pattern), tagging the route so the home-summary p95 is queryable alongside other tRPC procedures.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: apps/ai-worker/src/workers/ocr-worker.ts:285-297 `private async fetchDocument(url: string): Promise<Buffer> { ... const response = await fetch(url); ... }` — no `AbortController`/`signal`/timeout option. The method's own comment at line 286-290 states: '// In production, this would fetch from storage (S3, Supabase Storage, etc.) // For now, simulate with a placeholder ... // Simulated fetch - in production use actual HTTP client'.
findings.impact: As written today this is explicitly a stub, so production exposure is currently low/none — but if `OCREngine`/`ocr-worker.ts` is wired to a real job queue before this comment is addressed, an unresponsive source URL would hang a worker slot indefinitely with no bound, since Node's `fetch()` has no default timeout.
findings.rootCause: Placeholder implementation left without a timeout because it was never intended as the final integration point (per the code's own comment).
findings.policyViolation: CLAUDE.md 'Never Mock or Simulate Data' (the comment self-identifies this as simulated, which is a functional-completeness flag as much as a performance one) / ADR-018 (no timeout means no bounded worst-case latency)
findings.remediation: When this is wired to a real storage client, add an `AbortController` with a bounded timeout (mirroring apps/ai-worker/src/jobs/portal-sweep.job.ts:79-86's `const controller = new AbortController(); const timeout = setTimeout(() => controller.abort(), TIMEOUT_MS);` pattern already established elsewhere in the same app) before removing the 'simulated' comment.
findings.falsePositiveJustification: Code is explicitly self-documented as a placeholder/simulation, not a live production call path; the finding is precautionary for whenever it is wired to a real fetch.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: apps/ai-worker/src/lib/gemini-embeddings.ts:65-80 `const json = await this.caller.call(async () => { const res = await fetch(`${this.baseUrl}/models/${this.model}:embedContent`, { method: 'POST', headers: {...}, body: JSON.stringify({...}) }); ... });` — no `signal`/`AbortController`/timeout is passed to `fetch` itself. Contrast with apps/ai-worker/src/jobs/portal-sweep.job.ts:79-86, which explicitly builds an `AbortController` + `setTimeout(() => controller.abort(), TIMEOUT_MS)` and passes `signal: controller.signal` to its `fetch` call.
findings.impact: `this.caller` (LangChain's `AsyncCaller`, per the inline comment at line 64 'applies retry + concurrency limits') provides retry/concurrency control but is not guaranteed to enforce a hard per-request network timeout on the underlying `fetch` — a slow/hanging Gemini API response could hold a worker's concurrency slot for the platform default (which for `fetch` in Node is effectively unbounded) rather than the caller's own budget, delaying downstream embedding-dependent jobs (e.g. document indexing, semantic search backfills).
findings.rootCause: The raw `fetch` call was written without an explicit timeout, relying implicitly on the LangChain `AsyncCaller`'s retry wrapper for resilience rather than a request-level abort signal, unlike the sibling `portal-sweep.job.ts` in the same app which does set one explicitly.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-018-performance-load-testing.md (no bounded worst-case latency for this external dependency)
findings.remediation: Pass an `AbortController` with a bounded timeout (e.g. matching `portal-sweep.job.ts`'s `TIMEOUT_MS` pattern) into the `fetch()` call at gemini-embeddings.ts:66, independent of whatever retry policy `this.caller` already applies.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -rl "from 'recharts'" apps/web/src --include="*.tsx"` returns 13 files (RevenueTrendChart.tsx, ChurnTrendChart.tsx, LeadScoringTrendChart.tsx, SentimentTrend.tsx, LatencyTrendChart.tsx, ModelPerformanceChart.tsx, SavedReportView.tsx, DealsCharts.tsx, ForecastHistoryChart.tsx, ForecastRevenueChart.tsx, and 3 more). `grep -rl "next/dynamic" apps/web/src --include="*.tsx" | xargs grep -l "recharts"` returns only 2 files (apps/web/src/app/analytics/(list)/page.tsx and RevenueTrendChart.tsx itself), i.e. only the analytics-list route lazily code-splits its recharts-based chart; the other ~11 chart components (ai-intelligence, ai-monitoring, deals forecast) are eagerly imported by their parent pages.
findings.impact: recharts (and its d3-* dependencies) is a non-trivial client bundle addition; components importing it directly (not via `next/dynamic`) ship it in the initial JS chunk of whatever route renders them, rather than only when the chart is actually visible/needed. Cannot be quantified further without a build (see meta.unavailableMetrics).
findings.rootCause: Lazy-loading via `next/dynamic` was applied to the analytics list page specifically but not adopted as a project-wide convention for chart components.
findings.remediation: Wrap the remaining recharts-importing components (ai-intelligence/*, ai-monitoring/*, deals/DealsCharts.tsx, deals/forecast/ForecastHistoryChart.tsx, deals/ForecastRevenueChart.tsx) in `next/dynamic(() => import(...), { ssr: false })` at their call sites, matching the RevenueTrendChart.tsx precedent. Verify impact with `pnpm --filter @intelliflow/web build` + bundle analyzer (ADR-032 references Lighthouse >90 as the governing budget).
findings.falsePositiveJustification: Grep confirms the import pattern but not actual initial-route inclusion — some of these components may already sit behind route-level code splitting Next.js applies automatically per-page; a build/bundle-analyzer run is needed to confirm real impact, which this audit could not run.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: lead.router.ts:708 `const skip = (page - 1) * limit;` then lead.router.ts:730-753 `typedCtx.prismaWithTenant.lead.findMany({ where, skip, take: limit, ... })` paired with a parallel `.lead.count({ where })` — classic offset pagination, not cursor-based, unlike apps/api/src/modules/notifications/notifications.router.ts:342-346 which uses `take: limit + 1` with a `cursor` input (no `skip`).
findings.impact: Offset pagination degrades as `page` grows large (Postgres must scan and discard `skip` rows before returning `take` rows) — a real cost only at deep pagination depths that are uncommon in typical CRM list-browsing UX, but present if a tenant paginates far into a large lead list or an API consumer iterates pages programmatically.
findings.rootCause: The list endpoint predates or intentionally differs from the cursor-pagination pattern used by the notifications module; page-number UI (page 1, 2, 3...) is a harder fit for cursor pagination than infinite-scroll UIs like notifications.
findings.remediation: No action needed unless product requirements call for very deep pagination or bulk programmatic export; if so, offer a cursor-based variant (keyset pagination on `(createdAt, id)`, both already indexed per schema.prisma:492,497) alongside the existing page-number API rather than replacing it.
findings.falsePositiveJustification: This is a well-known, common, and often-acceptable tradeoff for page-numbered UIs at moderate depth; flagged for completeness rather than as confirmed customer-facing pain, since no evidence of actual deep-pagination usage was available to this audit.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: lead.router.ts:730-752 `findMany({ where, skip, take: limit, orderBy, include: { owner: { select: { id, email, name } }, contact: { select: { id, email, firstName, lastName } } } })` — related `owner`/`contact` rows are `select`-scoped, not full-row `include`. apps/api/src/modules/misc/global-search.router.ts:79-85,115-121,146,171-176,205-210,235-240 likewise pairs every `findMany` with an explicit `select` and a `take: limit` (max 10, enforced by `globalSearchInputSchema` at global-search.router.ts:32 `.max(10)`). apps/api/src/modules/notifications/notifications.router.ts:342-346 uses `take: limit + 1` cursor pagination.
findings.impact: None — recorded to close the audit scope item 'excessive payloads / unbounded findMany' with a positive finding: the highest-traffic list/search endpoints inspected (leads list, global search, notifications list) are already select-scoped and bounded. This contrasts with PERF-003/004 (search predicates lack index support) and PERF-005 (dashboard lacks caching), which remain real gaps.
findings.rootCause: n/a — compliant pattern, not a defect.
findings.remediation: No action required. Recommend this `select`-scoped + `take`-bounded pattern (lead.router.ts:730-752, global-search.router.ts) as the template when remediating PERF-003's search predicates.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: home.router.ts:1405-1504 contains 11 queries in one `Promise.all` (see PERF-005 for the full list) within a single tRPC procedure — under the ADR-053 default budget of 15 (packages/db/src/query-budget/config.ts:16 `export const DEFAULT_QUERY_BUDGET = 15;`) so it does NOT trip the automated over-budget reporter, but home.router.ts is 2588 lines with dozens of other findMany/count/aggregate call sites (grep count: 27 across the file) served by separate tRPC procedures that a single home-page render may call across multiple parallel tRPC batch requests (React Query / tRPC batching is not verifiable statically — see meta.unavailableMetrics).
findings.impact: Individually each procedure stays under budget, so ADR-053's detector — by design — will not flag this. Recorded as Medium/Info to note a blind spot in the current budget model: it is scoped per-tRPC-procedure (per `route`), not per-page-render, so a home page that fires several separate under-budget procedures in parallel has no aggregate ceiling.
findings.rootCause: ADR-053 explicitly scoped the budget to 'request-scoped' (one tRPC procedure invocation), which is the correct and intentional boundary per the ADR's stated design (context.ts seeds a fresh store per `runWithContext` call, i.e. per procedure) — this is not a bug in the detector, just a known boundary.
findings.remediation: No change recommended to the detector's scope (per-procedure is the right unit for the query-budget guard's stated purpose). If home-page aggregate load becomes a concern, address it via PERF-005's caching/consolidation remediation rather than widening the detector's scope.
findings.falsePositiveJustification: This is a description of an intentional design boundary (ADR-053), not a confirmed defect; included for audit completeness on the query-budget detector's actual coverage.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: packages/db/CLAUDE.md:5 states 'Prisma 7.4.2 with `engineType = "client"`', while the root CLAUDE.md's 'Context by Area' table (repo root CLAUDE.md, `packages/db/CLAUDE.md` row) still summarizes it as 'Prisma 5.22.0, NEVER --no-engine, tenantId rule'. This is a doc-drift observation surfaced while reading the two required context files for this audit (packages/db/CLAUDE.md and the task's own instructions), not a code defect.
findings.impact: Not a performance defect directly, but relevant context for this audit's own instructions (which cited 'Prisma 5.22.0 is used in packages/db/' as a compatibility note) — the live worktree is actually on Prisma 7.4.2 with the client-engine + `@prisma/adapter-pg` model, which changes connection-pooling behavior versus Prisma 5's binary engine and should be the baseline assumption for any future query-performance work in this package.
findings.rootCause: Root CLAUDE.md's per-area summary table was not updated when packages/db/CLAUDE.md was migrated to document the Prisma 7 upgrade.
findings.remediation: Update the root CLAUDE.md 'Context by Area' table's packages/db row to say 'Prisma 7.4.2 (client engine), NEVER --no-engine, tenantId rule' to match packages/db/CLAUDE.md:5. Out of scope for this performance audit to fix directly (doc-only, no code change), flagged for the docs-integrity workflow already active on this branch (per git status: docs-integrity-audit.ts/.github/workflows/docs-integrity.yml).
